title: Energy generation and supply systems and methods of operation
abstract: There is provided an energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) provides, when in operation, instantaneous frequency response, for example from a diesel standby plant and/or gas turbine engine plant. The supply system (50, 1000) beneficially includes a power generator (101, 102, 103, 1100, 1110) for generating power, an arrangement of energy storage devices (106, 107, 1120A, 1120B, 1130) for receiving power from the power generator (101, 103, 1100, 1110) and for buffering energy flows within the supply system (50, 1000), an energy management system (EMS) (114) for managing power flows within the supply system (50, 1000), and a power-flow metering arrangement (M) (109, 110, 111) for monitoring power flows occurring in respect of an electrical power grid (113) to which the supply system (50, 1000) is coupled, and an energy consuming arrangement (112, 1020) to which the supply system (50, 1000) is coupled. The energy management system (EMS) (114) is coupled to the power-flow metering arrangement (M) (109, 110, 111) for receiving information indicative of power flows occurring within the supply system (50, 1000), and the energy management system (EMS) (114) is coupled to control an inverter arrangement (105, 108) for interfacing electrical characteristics of the energy storage devices (106, 107, 1120A, 1120B, 1130) to the power generator (101, 102, 103, 1100, 1110), and for interfacing the energy storage devices (106, 107, 1120A, 1120B, 1130) to the electrical power grid (113) and the energy consuming arrangement (112, 1020). The supply system (50, 1000) is beneficially used to provide, when in operation, recharging of one or more electrical vehicles, for example for fast charging a plurality of electrical vehicles concurrently at a vehicle forecourt or service station.
claims:
Claims Claims 1. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system (1000) including an energy generation and supply system, characterized in that the electrical vehicle forecourt charging system (1000) includes one or more charging bays (1020) for receiving one or more electrical vehicles for recharging purposes, and a coupling arrangement for coupling the one or more electrical vehicles to the energy generation and supply system, wherein the energy generation and supply system is coupled to a source of energy (113, 1005) that supplies energy to the energy generation and supply system when in operation, wherein the energy generation and supply system includes one or more energy storage elements (103, 106, 107, 2010) therein for buffering energy flowing through the energy generation and supply system when in operation, wherein the energy generation and supply system includes one or more power generators (1100) therein for generating energy within the energy generation and supply system when in operation, wherein the energy generation and supply system includes a management arrangement (115, 116) that control power flows within the energy generation and supply system when in operation, wherein the energy generation and supply system provides recharging energy to one or more rechargeable batteries of the one or more electrical vehicles at least in part from at least one of the one or more energy storage elements, and the one or more power generators. 2. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) of claim 1, characterized in that the energy generation and supply system operates to provide demand response to the source of energy including an electrical power grid (113) that requires its generating capacity to be matched in real-time to power demand placed upon the electrical power grid (113). 3. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system (1000) of claim 2, characterized in that the one or more energy storage elements are implemented as one or more rechargeable batteries whose real-time state-of-charge (SOCreai-time) is adjusted to a nominal state-of-charge (SOCnom) by applying bias selectively to high excursions or low excursions of demand response provided by the energy generation and supply system to the electrical power grid (113). 4. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) of claim 1, 2 or 3, characterized in that the coupling arrangement is implemented as at least one of: (i) one or more cables; and (ii) one or more wireless inductive coupling apparatus. 5. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system (1000) of claim 1, 2, 3 or 4, characterized in that the one or more energy storage elements (106, 107) include a plurality of mutually different battery types, wherein the mutually different battery types have mutually different temporal discharge responses. 6. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) of claim 5, characterized in that the plurality of mutually different battery types includes at least one of: Lithium Iron Phosphate batteries, Metal hydride batteries, Magnesium salt flow batteries, supercapacitors, ultracapacitors. 7. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system (1000) of claim 5 or 6, characterized in that the one or more energy storage elements (103, 106, 107) are coupled within the system (1000) using one or more inverters (105, 108) that convert between DC and AC electrical operating regimes. 8. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) of any one of the preceding claims, characterized in that the one or more energy storage elements (106, 107) include a flywheel energy storage device, a compressed air energy storage arrangement (1120, 1130), a dynamometer arrangement ( 103), an arrangement of one or more thermal sources or sinks ( 1130). 9. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) of claim 8, characterized in that an arrangement of one or more thermal sources or sinks (1130) provides in operation Cogen functionality to provide, to a loality whereat the system (1000) is deployed, at least one of: heating, cooling, heating and cooling . 10. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) of claim 8, characterized in that the compressed air energy storage arrangement (1120, 1130) provides in operation one or more compressed air feeds for refilling compressed tanks of air-propelled vehicles parked at one or more bays (1020) of the system ( 1000). 11. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) of any one of the preceding claims, characterized in that the one or more power generators include at least one of: (i) one or more gas turbine engines; (ii) one or more diesel engines; (iii) one or more photovoltaic panels; (iv) one or more wind turbines; and (v) one or more heliostats. 12. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) of any one of the preceding claims, characterized in that the system (1000) includes an invertor arrangement (105, 108) that, when in operation, handles reactive power arising within the system (1000) in respect of energy storage or 

 supply provided by the one or more energy storage elements ( 103, 106, 107, 2010) . 13. A method of operating an electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) including an energy generation and supply system, characterized in that the method includes: (i) using one or more charging bays (1020) of the electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) for receiving one or more electrical vehicles for recharging purposes; (ii) using a coupling arrangement for coupling the one or more electrical vehicles to the energy generation and supply system; (iii) arranging for the energy generation and supply system to be coupled to a source of energy ( 113, 1005) that supplies energy to the energy generation and supply system when in operation, (iv) using one or more energy storage elements (103, 106, 107, 2010) of the energy generation and supply system to buffer in operation energy flowing through the energy generation and supply system; (v) using one or more power generators (1100) of the energy generation and supply system to generate in operation energy within the energy generation and supply system; (vi) using a management arrangement ( 115, 116) to control in operation power flows within the energy generation and supply system, wherein the energy generation and supply system provides recharging energy to one or more rechargeable batteries of the one or more electrical vehicles at least in part from at least one of the one or more energy storage elements, and the one or more power generators. 14. A method of claim 13, characterized in that the method includes arranging for the energy generation and supply system to provide demand response in operation to the source of energy including an electrical power 

 grid (113) that requires its generating capacity to be matched in real-time to power demand placed upon the electrical power grid (113). 15. A method of claim 14, characterized in that the method includes implementing the one or more energy storage elements as one or more rechargeable batteries whose real-time state-of-charge (SOCreai-time) is adjusted to a nominal state-of-charge (SOCnom) by applying bias selectively to high excursions or low excursions of demand response provided by the energy generation and supply system to the electrical power grid (113). 16. A method of claim 13, 14 or 15, characterized in that the coupling arrangement is implemented as at least one of: (i) one or more cables; and (ii) one or more wireless inductive coupling apparatus. 17. A method of claim 13, 14, 15 or 16, characterized in that the one or more energy storage elements (106, 107) include a plurality of mutually different battery types, wherein the mutually different battery types have mutually different temporal discharge responses. 18. A method of claim 17, characterized in that the plurality of mutually different battery types includes at least one of: Lithium Iron Phosphate batteries, Metal hydride batteries, Magnesium salt flow batteries, supercapacitors, ultracapacitors. 19. A method of claim 18, characterized in that the one or more energy storage elements (103, 106, 107) are coupled within the system (1000) using one or more inverters (105, 108) that convert between DC and AC electrical operating regimes. 20. A method of any one of the preceding claims 13 to 19, characterized in that the one or more energy storage elements ( 106, 107) include a flywheel energy storage device, a compressed air energy storage arrangement ( 1120, 1130), a dynamometer arrangement (103), an arrangement of one or more thermal sources or sinks (1130). 21. A method of claim 20, characterized in that an arrangement of one or more thermal sources or sinks (1 130) provides in operation Cogen functionality to provide, to a locality whereat the system ( 1000) is deployed, at least one of: heating, cooling, heating and cooling . 22. A method of claim 21, characterized in that the compressed air energy storage arrangement (1120, 1130) provides in operation one or more compressed air feeds for refilling compressed tanks of air-propelled vehicles parked at one or more bays ( 1020) of the system ( 1000) . 23. A method of any one of claims 13 to 22, characterized in that the one or more power generators include at least one of: (i) one or more gas turbine engines; (ii) one or more diesel engines; (iii) one or more photovoltaic panels; (iv) one or more wind turbines; and (v) one or more heliostats. 24. A method of any one of claims 13 to 24, characterized in that the method includes arrangement for the system (1000) to include an i nvertor arrangement (105, 108) that, when in operation, handles reactive power arising within the system (1000) in respect of energy storage or supply provided by the one or more energy storage elements (103, 106, 107, 2010) . 25. An energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) including a power generator ( 101, 102, 103) for generating power, an arrangement of energy storage devices ( 106, 107) for receiving power from the power generator ( 101, 103) and for buffering energy flows within the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000), an energy management system (EMS) ( 114) for managing power flows within the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000), and a power-flow metering arrangement (M) (109, 110, 111) for monitoring power flows occurring in respect of an electrical power grid ( 113) to which the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) is coupled, and an energy consuming arrangement (112, 1120) to which the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) is coupled, characterized in that the energy management system (EMS) (114) is coupled to the power-flow metering arrangement (M) ( 109, 110, 111) for receiving information indicative of power flows occurring within the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000), and the energy management system (EMS) ( 114) is coupled to control an invertor arrangement ( 105, 108) for interfacing electrical characteristics of the energy storage devices ( 106, 107) to the power generator (101, 102, 103), and for interfacing the energy storage devices ( 106, 107) to the electrical power grid ( 113) and the energy consuming arrangement ( 112). 26. An energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) of claim 25, characterized in that the energy storage devices ( 106, 107) include a combination of one or more primary energy storage devices ( 106) for accommodating rapid power flows and one or more secondary devices (107) for accommodating slower power flows. 27. An energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) of claim 26, characterized in that the rapid power flows occur within a period of less than 1 Second, and the slower power flows occur within a period of more than 1 Second. 28. An energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) of any one of preceding claims 25 to 27, characterized in that the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) includes a resistive load arrangement (116, 117) that is couplable, via control of the energy management system (EMS) ( 114), for use in adjusting a state-of-charge (SOC) of the energy storage devices ( 106, 107) . 29. An energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) of any one of claims 25 to 28, characterized in that the invertor arrangement (105, 108) is operable to handle reactive power arising within the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) . 30. An energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) of any one of preceding claims 25 to 29, characterized in that the power generator (101, 102, 103) includes an internal combustion engine arrangement (101) whose mechanical output is coupled to a dynamometer arrangement ( 103) for generating electrical power for use in charging the energy storage devices ( 106, 107). 31. An energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) of any one of preceding claims 25 to 30, characterized in that the energy consuming arrangement ( 112) is at least one of: a factory, an electrical vehicle recharging facility. 32. An energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) of claim 31 , characterized in that the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) is coupled to the energy consuming arrangement ( 112) via a wireless resonant inductive power coupling arrangement, for electrical vehicle recharging purposes. 33. An energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) of any one of preceding claims 25 to 32, characterized in that the energy generation 

 and supply system (50, 1000) is configured to provide response load services to at least one of: the electrical power grid ( 113), the energy consuming arrangement (112) . 34. An energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) of claim 33, characterized in that the response load services include frequency response services for providing power flow as a function of frequency to and/or from the at least one of: the electrical power grid ( 113), the energy consuming arrangement ( 112) . 35. A method of using an energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) including a power generator ( 101, 102, 103) for generating power, an arrangement of energy storage devices ( 106, 107) for receiving power from the power generator ( 101, 102, 103) and for buffering energy flows within the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000), an energy management system (EMS) ( 114) for managing power flows within the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000), and a power-flow metering arrangement (M) ( 109, 110, 111) for monitoring power flows occurring in respect of an electrical power grid ( 113) to which the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) is coupled, and an energy consuming arrangement ( 112) to which the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) is coupled, characterized in that the method includes: (i) coupling the energy management system (EMS) ( 114) to the power- flow metering arrangement (M) ( 109, 110, 111) for receiving information indicative of power flows occurring within the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) ; and (ii) coupling the energy management system (EMS) ( 114) to control an invertor arrangement ( 105, 108) for interfacing electrical characteristics of the energy storage devices ( 106, 107) to the power generator ( 101, 102, 103), and for interfacing the energy storage devices ( 106, 107) to the electrical power grid ( 113) and the energy consuming arrangement ( 112) . 36. A method of claim 35, characterized in that the method includes arranging for the energy storage devices ( 106, 107) to include a combination of one or more primary energy storage devices ( 106) for accommodating rapid power flows and one or more secondary devices (107) for accommodating slower power flows. 37. A method of claim 36, characterized in that the method includes accommodating rapid power flows occurring within a period of less than 1 Second, and slower power flows occurring within a period of more than 1 Second. 38. A method of any one of claims 35 to 37, characterized in that the method includes arranging for the energy supply system to include a resistive load arrangement that is couplable, via control of the energy management system (EMS) ( 114), for use in adjusting a state of charge (SOC) of the energy storage devices ( 106, 107). 39. A method of any one of claims 35 to 38, characterized in that the method includes operating the invertor arrangement ( 105, 108) to handle reactive power arising within the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) . 40. A method of any one of claims 35 to 39, characterized in that the method includes arranging for the power generator ( 101, 102, 103) to include an internal combustion engine arrangement ( 101) whose mechanical output is coupled to a dynamometer arrangement ( 103) for generating electrical power for use in charging the energy storage devices (106, 107) . 41. A method of any one of preceding claims 35 to 40, characterized in that the method includes arranging for the energy consuming 

 arrangement to be at least one of: a factory, an electrical vehicle recharging facility. 42. A method of claim 41 , characterized in that the method includes arranging for the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) to be coupled to the energy consuming arrangement ( 1 12, 1 120) via a wireless resonant inductive power coupling arrangement, for electrical vehicle recharging purposes. 43. A method of any one of claims 35 to 42, characterized in that the energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) is configured to provide response load services to at least one of: the electrical power grid ( 1 13), the energy consuming arrangement ( 1 12) . 44. A method of claim 43, characterized in that the response load services include frequency response services for provide power flow as a function of frequency to and/or from the at least one of: the electrical power grid ( 1 13), the energy consuming arrangement ( 1 12) . 45. An electrical vehicle forecourt charging system ( 1000) including an energy generation and supply system (50, 1000) of any one of claims 35 to 44, wherein the energy generation and supply system is operable to provide power for electrical vehicle battery recharging purposes. 46. A computer program products comprising non-transitory computer- readable storage media having computer-readable instructions stored thereon, the computer-readable instructions being executable by a computerized device comprising processing hardware to execute a method of any one of claims 13 to 24, or 35 to 45.
